title: Is underfloor heating cheaper to run than radiators?
description: Wet underfloor heating can run 5 to 25% cheaper than radiators thanks to lower flow temperatures, but electric mats cost far more. The real UK maths.
blurb: Wet underfloor heating can undercut radiators, while electric mats can triple your heating bill. Here is why.
lede: Underfloor heating has a reputation as a luxury, which makes people assume it must be expensive to run. The truth is more interesting. A wet underfloor system fed by your boiler or a heat pump usually costs slightly less to run than radiators in the same house, because it works at much lower water temperatures and lets you feel warm at a lower thermostat setting. An electric underfloor system, on the other hand, is one of the most expensive ways to heat a room in Britain, because every unit of heat comes from electricity at more than three times the price of gas. The word underfloor covers both, and the running costs sit at opposite ends of the scale, so the first job is to be clear about which one you are pricing up.
short_answer: Wet underfloor heating is usually a little cheaper to run than radiators on the same boiler, typically 5 to 10% less, because the low flow temperature keeps a condensing boiler in its most efficient range and the radiant warmth lets you set the thermostat 1 to 2 degrees lower. Paired with a heat pump the gap widens to 20% or more. Electric underfloor mats are the exception: at 26.11p per kWh they cost over three times as much per unit of heat as a gas boiler, so they only make sense in small rooms used for short periods.

sections.h2: Wet and electric underfloor heating are completely different propositions
sections.html:
Two technologies share the name, and the cost difference between them is enormous.

  • Wet (hydronic) underfloor heating circulates warm water through pipes buried in the floor. The heat comes from your gas boiler or heat pump, so each kWh of heat costs roughly what your central heating already costs, and often a little less because of the efficiency gains covered below.
  • Electric underfloor heating uses resistive mats or cables under the floor finish. Every kWh of heat is a kWh of electricity at the full unit rate, currently 26.11p under the price cap, against 7.33p for gas. It is essentially a flat electric heater you stand on.

So when someone says underfloor heating is cheap to run, they almost always mean a wet system. When someone says it ruined their bills, they almost always mean electric mats running for hours in a large room. We cover the mats in detail in our electric underfloor heating running cost guide; the rest of this page compares like with like, meaning a wet system against radiators on the same heat source.

sections.h2: Why wet underfloor heating beats radiators on efficiency
sections.html:
A radiator is a small, hot surface. To push enough heat into a room it typically needs water at 60 to 70°C. A floor is a huge surface, often twenty times the area of the radiators it replaces, so it can deliver the same heat with water at just 35 to 45°C. That low flow temperature pays off twice.

1. Your condensing boiler actually condenses

A modern gas boiler only hits its advertised 90%+ efficiency when the water returning to it is below about 55°C, cool enough for the flue gases to condense and give up their latent heat. Radiators run hot enough that many boilers spend most of the winter outside this zone, working at 80 to 85%. Underfloor return temperatures of 30 to 40°C keep the boiler condensing all the time, worth roughly 5 to 10% off the gas used for the same heat. It is the same physics that makes turning down your boiler flow temperature one of the best free savings available with radiators.

2. You feel warm at a lower air temperature

Underfloor heating warms you partly by radiant heat from below and keeps the warmth spread evenly from floor to ceiling, instead of pooling hot air near the ceiling the way convection from radiators does. Most people report feeling comfortable with the thermostat set 1 to 2°C lower than they needed with radiators. Each degree off the thermostat trims heating energy by something like 10%, so this comfort effect is often worth as much as the boiler efficiency gain.

Stack the two together and a well designed wet system typically uses 10 to 15% less fuel than radiators delivering the same comfort, though a slow, poorly controlled system in a leaky house can hand much of that back, as the next sections show.

sections.h2: The running costs side by side
sections.html:
Take a typical three bed semi needing around 10,000kWh of useful heat a year, close to the UK average for space heating. Here is what delivering that heat costs with each combination, using realistic seasonal efficiencies.

SystemEfficiency / COPEnergy boughtAnnual running cost
Gas boiler + radiators (typical setup)85%11,765kWh gas£862
Gas boiler + wet underfloor93%10,753kWh gas£788
Heat pump + radiatorsCOP 3.03,333kWh electricity£870
Heat pump + wet underfloorCOP 4.02,500kWh electricity£653
Electric underfloor mats throughout100%10,000kWh electricity£2,611

At 26.11p per kWh electricity and 7.33p per kWh gas, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Heat demand of 10,000kWh per year assumed; costs exclude standing charges, which you pay regardless of system.

Three things stand out. On gas, wet underfloor saves a worthwhile but modest £70 to £80 a year before any thermostat saving, which could add another £80 or so on top. On a heat pump, the low flow temperature lifts the COP enough to make underfloor clearly the cheapest option on the table. And whole house electric mats cost about three times as much as anything else, which is why they belong in bathrooms, not living rooms. For context on what heating costs across different house types, see our guide to the cost of heating a UK home, part of our wider heating section.

Running cost is not the whole story, and radiators keep some genuine advantages.

  • Response time. Radiators warm a room in 20 to 30 minutes. Underfloor pipes buried in 65mm of screed can take two to four hours to bring a cold room up, and just as long to cool down. That thermal lag means underfloor systems are usually run for long, steady periods with setback temperatures rather than switched on and off, which suits people at home all day but wastes heat in a house empty from 8am to 6pm unless the controls are programmed carefully.
  • Leaky houses. The efficiency case assumes the floor can supply the room's heat loss at low temperature. In a draughty, poorly insulated house the floor may not keep up on the coldest days, and long run times in a building that leaks heat erode the savings. Insulation first, emitters second.
  • Installation cost. Retrofitting a wet system means lifting floors, adding insulation below the pipes, and often raising floor levels. £100 to £150 per square metre installed is typical for retrofit, against £200 to £400 to replace a radiator. The running cost saving of £100 to £150 a year on gas will not repay a £6,000 retrofit in any sensible timeframe. In a new build or full renovation, where the floor is open anyway, the premium shrinks dramatically and the maths changes.
  • Simple zoning already works. Thermostatic valves let you zone radiators room by room for almost nothing, capturing a good slice of the targeted-heating saving without touching the floor.

sections.h2: The strongest case: underfloor heating with a heat pump
sections.html:
If you are planning a heat pump, underfloor heating moves from nice-to-have to near ideal partner. A heat pump's efficiency depends directly on how little it has to lift the temperature: the cooler the water it produces, the more units of heat it delivers per unit of electricity. Asking it for 60°C radiator water might yield a COP around 2.5; asking it for 35°C underfloor water can push the COP to 4 or beyond. That is the difference between heat costing about 10.4p per kWh and about 6.5p per kWh, taking it from slightly dearer than gas to clearly cheaper.

This is why heat pump installers fit oversized radiators when underfloor is not an option: the goal is always the lowest flow temperature the emitters allow. Our heat pumps explained guide covers how the technology works, and the heat pump versus gas boiler comparison runs the full numbers. The thermal mass of a heated floor also suits time of use tariffs: the screed can be charged with cheap overnight electricity and release heat through the morning, something radiators cannot do.

Wet underfloor heating is genuinely cheaper to run than radiators, typically by 5 to 10% on a gas boiler and 20% or more on a heat pump, with the comfort effect of a lower thermostat setting adding to that. But the saving is measured in tens of pounds a year on gas, so it never justifies ripping up sound floors on running cost alone. Fit it when the floor is already open, when you are building, extending or renovating, and especially when a heat pump is in the plan, because that is where the low flow temperature earns real money year after year.

Electric underfloor mats are a different product wearing the same name. Reserve them for a small bathroom run an hour a day on a timer, where the cost is pence, and never use them as the main heat source for a large room. And whichever side of the debate you land on, the cheapest improvements come first: a boiler flow temperature turned down, rooms zoned sensibly, and a house that holds its heat will save more than any change of emitter.

faq.q: Is underfloor heating cheaper to run than radiators on a gas boiler?
faq.a: Usually yes, by around 5 to 10%. The low water temperature keeps a condensing boiler at peak efficiency, and the even radiant warmth lets most people set the thermostat 1 to 2 degrees lower for the same comfort. On a typical three bed semi that is worth roughly £70 to £150 a year at current gas prices.

faq.q: Why is electric underfloor heating so expensive to run?
faq.a: Electric mats convert electricity directly to heat, so every kWh costs the full electricity unit rate of 26.11p under the July to September 2026 price cap. Gas costs 7.33p per kWh, so an electric floor delivers heat at more than three times the price of a boiler fed wet system. A 150W per square metre mat in a 4 square metre bathroom costs about 16p an hour, fine on a timer, but heating whole rooms this way gets costly fast.

faq.q: Should I leave underfloor heating on all the time?
faq.a: Not at full temperature, but underfloor systems in screed respond slowly, so they work best running long steady hours with a setback of 3 to 4 degrees overnight or when you are out, rather than switching fully off. A full cool down can take half a day to recover from, which forces the system to run hard and cancels the saving. Timber and low profile systems respond faster and can be timed more like radiators.

faq.q: Does underfloor heating work better with a heat pump than radiators do?
faq.a: Yes, and it is the best pairing available. A heat pump producing 35°C water for underfloor heating can reach a COP of around 4, against roughly 2.5 to 3 when making 55 to 60°C water for radiators. That lifts efficiency by a third or more and typically makes the heat pump clearly cheaper to run than a gas boiler.

faq.q: Is it worth retrofitting wet underfloor heating to save money?
faq.a: On running cost alone, no. Retrofit costs of £100 to £150 per square metre against a saving of £100 to £150 a year on gas means payback measured in decades. It becomes worthwhile when floors are already up during a renovation or extension, or as part of a heat pump installation where the low flow temperature delivers a much larger efficiency gain.
